tier_2_handbook.chondrosarcoma_definition: Chondrosarcomas are malignant bone tumors that characteristically produce cartilage matrices from neoplastic tissue devoid of osteoid. They may occur at any age but are more common in older adults, with peak incidence in individuals >50 years [6]. The most common primary sites are the pelvis and proximal femur [61,62]. Conventional chondrosarcoma (CCS) constitutes approximately 90% of all chondrosarcomas, and of these, 90% are low to intermediate grade [65]. Low-grade (grade 1) tumors in the appendicular skeleton are classified by the World Health Organization (WHO) as atypical cartilaginous tumors (low-grade intracompartmental appendicular tumors), while those in the axial skeleton are considered grade 1 chondrosarcomas [5]. Non-conventional subtypes, including clear cell, juxtacortical, dedifferentiated, myxoid, and mesenchymal forms, constitute about 10% to 15% of all chondrosarcomas [61,71,72]. Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma is a separate soft tissue sarcoma entity with distinct chromosomal translocations, and its management is addressed in the NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma [76,77]. Symptoms are usually mild and depend on tumor size and location; patients with pelvic or axial lesions often present later due to insidious pain [80-82].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Conventional chondrosarcoma (CCS)
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: Most common subtype, producing cartilage matrix; nearly 90% of all chondrosarcomas. Grades 1–3, with 90% low to intermediate grade. Central (medullary) or peripheral (periosteal) origin.
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: IDH1/IDH2 mutations in ~65% of cases; nearly all cases of Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome are related to IDH1/IDH2 mutations [65,68-70]. Inactivating mutations of CDKN2A and COL2A1 [64].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Atypical cartilaginous tumor (ACT)
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: WHO classification for low-grade (grade 1) intracompartmental appendicular tumors. Previously termed grade I intracompartmental chondrosarcoma. Limited metastatic potential.
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.frequency: Represents low-grade CCS in appendicular skeleton; exact frequency not specified but constitutes a subset of CCS.
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: IDH mutations common; good prognosis with intralesional excision [99,100].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: High-grade subtype with both a cartilaginous component and a high-grade non-cartilaginous sarcoma component. Most aggressive; median survival 11 months [72].
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: Retinoblastoma pathway alterations common [71]. PD-L1 expression in 41% [135]. May be treated with osteosarcoma regimens (category 2B) [BONE-B 1 of 7].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: Biphasic tumor with small round cells and islands of hyaline cartilage. Can be skeletal (40%) or extraskeletal (60%) [75].
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: Often has HEY1-NCOA2 fusion (not explicitly in source but implied by Ewing-like regimens). Retinoblastoma pathway alterations [71]. Better outcome in younger patients who receive chemotherapy [128].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Clear cell chondrosarcoma
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: Low-grade malignant tumor with clear cells and minimal cartilage matrix. Usually arises in the femoral head.
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.frequency: Rare; constitutes a small fraction of non-conventional subtypes.

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Juxtacortical (periosteal) chondrosarcoma
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: Arises on the surface of bone, usually low grade. Excellent prognosis; median survival 97 months [72].
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.frequency: Least associated with metastasis at presentation (2.1%) [72].

chondrosarcoma_subtypes.name: Myxoid chondrosarcoma of bone
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.description: Myxoid variant of intermediate- or high-grade chondrosarcoma, commonly located in bones around the hip joint [61,74]. Extremely rare.
chondrosarcoma_subtypes.frequency: Extremely rare; not fully characterized as distinct entity [73,74].

tier_2_handbook.chondrosarcoma_molecular_pathogenesis: The pathogenesis of chondrosarcoma is driven by recurrent genetic alterations. IDH1 and IDH2 mutations are found in approximately 65% of conventional central chondrosarcoma cases and in nearly all cases of Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome [65,68-70]. These mutations lead to the production of the oncometabolite 2-hydroxyglutarate (2-HG). Inactivating mutations of CDKN2A and COL2A1 have also been implicated [64]. Non-conventional subtypes (clear cell, dedifferentiated, mesenchymal) frequently harbor alterations in the retinoblastoma pathway [71]. Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma may express PD-L1 in 41% of cases [135]. Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma is characterized by a HEY1-NCOA2 fusion (not explicitly stated but referenced in Ewing-like treatment context). Extraskeletal myxoid chondrosarcoma carries specific chromosomal translocations such as t(9;22)(q22;q11-12) generating EWSR1-NR4A3 fusion, but this is a soft tissue sarcoma, not primary bone [76,77]. The molecular landscape of chondrosarcoma offers potential targeted therapy opportunities, such as ivosidenib for IDH1-mutant tumors [133].
tier_2_handbook.chondrosarcoma_epidemiology: Chondrosarcoma is the most common primary bone cancer in adults, accounting for >40% of newly diagnosed bone cancers in the United States [3]. In 2025, an estimated 3,770 people will be diagnosed with primary bone cancer (all types) and 2,190 will die [2]. Chondrosarcoma is more prevalent in individuals >50 years of age [6]. Analysis of 2,890 patients from the SEER database (1973–2003) showed that female sex, low histologic grade, and local surgical stage were associated with better disease-specific survival on univariate analysis; grade and stage remained significant on multivariate analysis [89]. Five-year relative survival across all bone cancers is 68% [1]. For mesenchymal chondrosarcoma, 5- and 10-year overall survival (OS) are 51% and 43%, respectively; axial tumor location portends poorer OS [75]. Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma has the worst prognosis with median survival of 11 months, while juxtacortical subtype has the highest median survival at 97 months [72]. Metastasis at presentation occurs most frequently in dedifferentiated (19.8%) and mesenchymal (10.6%) subtypes [72].

chondrosarcoma_risk_factors.detail: Multiple enchondromas; risk of malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma. Nearly all cases related to IDH1/IDH2 mosaic mutations [66].
chondrosarcoma_risk_factors.strength: Strong; ~65% of chondrosarcoma cases and nearly all Ollier/Maffucci associated with IDH mutations [65,68-70].

chondrosarcoma_risk_factors.detail: Enchondromatosis with soft tissue hemangiomas; high risk of malignant transformation to chondrosarcoma.
chondrosarcoma_risk_factors.strength: Strong; same IDH mutation association as Ollier [66].

chondrosarcoma_risk_factors.detail: Secondary chondrosarcomas can arise from enchondromas or osteochondromas (cartilaginous cap >2 cm or growth after skeletal maturity raises suspicion) [83].

tier_2_handbook.chordoma_definition: Chordomas are rare malignant bone tumors that arise from embryonic remnants of the notochord. They occur most commonly in adults aged 40–75 years, with a male predominance [7-9]. The primary sites are the sacrum (50–60%), skull base (25–35%), and mobile spine (15%) [8,9,140]. Chordomas are traditionally classified by the WHO into three histologic variants: conventional (most common), chondroid (5–15%), and dedifferentiated (2–8%) [140]. An additional subtype, poorly differentiated chordoma, is characterized by loss of SMARCB1 expression and occurs more frequently in children, with predilection for skull base and cervical spine [141,142]. Benign notochordal cell tumors (BNCTs) are considered precursor lesions and do not require surgical management [145-147]. Symptoms include localized deep pain, radiculopathies, or neurologic deficits depending on location; cervical chordomas may cause airway obstruction or dysphagia [8,143].

chordoma_subtypes.name: Conventional chordoma
chordoma_subtypes.description: Most common histologic subtype, characterized by physaliphorous cells in a myxoid stroma. Absence of cartilaginous or mesenchymal components. Expresses brachyury.
chordoma_subtypes.key_associations: Associated with brachyury expression; notochordal origin.

chordoma_subtypes.name: Chondroid chordoma
chordoma_subtypes.description: Contains histologic features of both chordoma and cartilage elements.
chordoma_subtypes.key_associations: Better prognosis than conventional? Not explicitly stated, but considered.

chordoma_subtypes.name: Dedifferentiated chordoma
chordoma_subtypes.description: Features of high-grade pleomorphic spindle cell soft tissue sarcoma. Most aggressive clinical course.
chordoma_subtypes.key_associations: Poor prognosis; managed as per NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma [CHOR-1].

chordoma_subtypes.name: Poorly differentiated chordoma
chordoma_subtypes.description: Molecularly characterized by absence of SMARCB1 expression. More common in pediatric population; predilection for skull base and cervical spine. Aggressive behavior with poorer overall survival [141,142].
chordoma_subtypes.key_associations: SMARCB1 loss; often requires aggressive multimodal therapy.

tier_2_handbook.chordoma_molecular_pathogenesis: Chordomas arise from notochordal remnants, with brachyury (TBXT) as a key immunohistochemical marker. Conventional and chondroid subtypes typically retain SMARCB1 expression. Poorly differentiated chordoma is defined by loss of SMARCB1 (INI1) expression, a chromatin remodeling gene, leading to aggressive behavior [141,142]. Several signal transduction pathways have been implicated in pathogenesis, including PDGFR, EGFR, and mTOR [209-211]. Activating mutations or amplifications in these pathways may provide therapeutic targets. Dedifferentiated chordoma likely shares molecular features with high-grade sarcomas.
tier_2_handbook.chordoma_epidemiology: Chordoma accounts for approximately 10% of primary bone cancers [3]. Incidence peaks in the fifth to sixth decade of life, with a male predominance [7-9]. In the United States, chordoma occurs in about 1 in 1 million people per year. SEER data indicate that overall 5-year survival varies by location and resectability. Skull base chordomas have slightly better outcomes than sacral/spinal chordomas. Male sex has been associated with worse progression-free and overall survival in skull base chordomas [144].

chordoma_risk_factors.detail: Chordomas arise from remnant notochordal tissue; no specific environmental risk factors identified.

tier_2_handbook.ewing_sarcoma_definition: Ewing sarcoma is a highly malignant primary bone tumor characterized by a fusion of the EWSR1 gene with an ETS family transcription factor (most commonly FLI1) [12,13]. It belongs to the Ewing sarcoma family of tumors, which also includes primitive neuroectodermal tumor (PNET) of bone, Askin tumor, and extraosseous Ewing sarcoma [c]. Approximately 90% of Ewing sarcomas have one of four specific chromosome translocations [g]. Ewing sarcoma primarily affects children, adolescents, and young adults, with an incidence of 1 case per 1.5 million [236]. The most common primary sites are pelvic bones, femur, and chest wall bones [26]. When arising in long bones, the diaphysis is most frequently affected. Periosteal reaction appears as "onion skin" on radiographs. Other primary round cell sarcomas of bone (e.g., CIC::DUX4, BCOR::CCNB3) are now recognized as distinct entities but can be treated similarly [g].

ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.name: Classic Ewing sarcoma
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.description: Small round cell tumor with EWSR1-FLI1 fusion (85%) or other ETS family fusion. Strong expression of CD99 [231,232].
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.frequency: ~85% of Ewing sarcomas have EWSR1-FLI1; overall Ewing sarcoma incidence 1/1.5 million [236]

ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.name: Other EWSR1-ETS fusions
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.description: EWSR1 fused with ERG, ETV1, ETV4, or FEV. Prognostic significance controversial; current effective therapies yield similar outcomes [264,265].

ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.name: FUS-rearranged Ewing sarcoma
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.description: Rare cases where FUS substitutes for EWSR1, resulting in FUS-ERG (t(16;21)) or FUS-FEV (t(2;16)) [229,230].
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: Consider FUS in molecular diagnosis when EWSR1 negative [g].

ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.name: Other primary round cell sarcomas of bone
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.description: Previously called Ewing-like sarcomas. Include CIC-rearranged (CIC::DUX4), BCOR-rearranged (BCOR::CCNB3), and EWSR1 fusions with non-ETS genes. Do not have canonical EWSR1-ETS fusions [234,235].
ewing_sarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: May be treated like Ewing sarcoma [g].

tier_2_handbook.ewing_sarcoma_molecular_pathogenesis: The molecular hallmark of Ewing sarcoma is a reciprocal chromosomal translocation fusing the EWSR1 gene (22q12) with a member of the ETS family of transcription factors. The most common is t(11;22)(q24;q12) producing EWSR1-FLI1 fusion, found in ~85% of cases [12,15]. Less common fusions include EWSR1-ERG, EWSR1-ETV1, EWSR1-ETV4, EWSR1-FEV, and rare FUS-ERG or FUS-FEV fusions [12,13,229,230]. These chimeric proteins act as aberrant transcription factors driving oncogenesis. Strong expression of MIC2 (CD99) is characteristic [231,232]. Other primary round cell sarcomas lack EWSR1-ETS fusions and instead harbor CIC::DUX4, BCOR::CCNB3, or other rearrangements [234,235].
tier_2_handbook.ewing_sarcoma_epidemiology: Ewing sarcoma accounts for approximately 8% of primary bone cancers [3]. It occurs predominantly in children and adolescents, with an incidence of 1 case per 1.5 million [236]. The most common primary sites are pelvis, femur, and chest wall [26]. Approximately 20% of cases arise in extraosseous tissue [236]. Five-year relative survival for localized Ewing sarcoma has improved to 82% with modern multimodality therapy [31]. Survival for metastatic disease remains poor, with 5-year event-free survival of ~22% [32,268]. Risk factors for poorer outcome include pelvic/axial primary site, tumor volume >100 mL, elevated LDH, and presence of metastases at diagnosis [237-244]. In a SEER analysis, adult age, Hispanic ancestry, metastatic disease, large tumor size, and low socioeconomic status were associated with worse survival [255].

ewing_sarcoma_risk_factors.detail: Peak incidence in adolescents and young adults; adult age (>18) associated with worse prognosis [255].
SEER data show Hispanic ancestry as poor prognostic factor for overall survival [255].
Pelvic or axial primary site associated with worse outcome compared to extremity [245].
Single most significant adverse prognostic factor; 5-year RFS 22% vs 55% for localized [32].

tier_2_handbook.giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_definition: Giant cell tumor of bone (GCTB) is a rare, benign primary bone tumor that is locally aggressive and rarely metastasizes to the lungs. It accounts for about 3% to 5% of all primary bone tumors [339,340]. GCTB usually occurs between 20 and 40 years of age, with a slight female predominance [341]. The most common sites are the meta-epiphyseal regions of the distal femur and proximal tibia [341]. Histologically, GCTB is characterized by numerous osteoclast-like giant cells in a background of mononuclear stromal cells. Malignant transformation to high-grade osteosarcoma is exceptionally rare but carries a poor prognosis [342,343]. Brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism is an important differential diagnosis [346].

giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_subtypes.name: Conventional GCTB
giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_subtypes.description: Benign but locally aggressive tumor with osteoclast-like giant cells and mononuclear stromal cells. Can cause significant bone destruction.
giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_subtypes.key_associations: RANKL expression by stromal cells; responds to denosumab [389-395].

giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_subtypes.name: Malignant GCTB
giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_subtypes.description: Rare malignant transformation to high-grade osteosarcoma. Poor prognosis [342,343]. May be associated with prior radiation or denosumab therapy [404-406].

tier_2_handbook.giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_molecular_patho: GCTB is characterized by abundant RANKL expression on neoplastic mononuclear stromal cells, which stimulates RANK-expressing osteoclast precursors to form osteoclast-like giant cells. This RANK-RANKL interaction drives osteolysis. Denosumab, a humanized monoclonal antibody against RANKL, effectively inhibits this pathway [389-395]. No specific driver mutations are consistently identified; however, malignant transformation may involve acquisition of osteosarcoma-related genomic alterations.
tier_2_handbook.giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_epidemiology: GCTB accounts for 3% to 5% of all primary bone tumors [339,340]. It typically occurs in young adults aged 20–40 years [341]. The most common location is around the knee (distal femur, proximal tibia). Lung metastases occur in a small percentage of cases (typically <5%); the average time to metastasis ranges from 2 to 4 years [417-419]. Malignant transformation is very rare.

giant_cell_tumor_of_bone_risk_factors.detail: No specific environmental or genetic risk factors are known for the development of GCTB.

tier_2_handbook.osteosarcoma_definition: Osteosarcoma is the most common primary malignant bone tumor in children and young adults, with a bimodal age distribution peaking at ages 10–14 years and again after age 65 [420]. It is a spindle cell or pleomorphic tumor that produces osteoid or immature bone. High-grade intramedullary osteosarcoma (conventional type) comprises nearly 80% of all osteosarcomas, most frequently arising in the metaphysis of the distal femur or proximal tibia [421]. Low-grade intramedullary osteosarcoma accounts for <2% [422]. Surface (juxtacortical) variants include parosteal (low-grade, <5%), periosteal (intermediate-grade), and high-grade surface osteosarcoma (rare) [422,425,426]. Extraskeletal osteosarcoma is managed according to NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma [OSTEO-1]. High-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) of bone can be treated using osteosarcoma algorithms (category 2B) [BONE-B 4 of 7]. Osteosarcoma spreads hematogenously, most commonly to lungs. Pain and swelling are frequent early symptoms, sometimes mistaken for growing pains [430].

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: High-grade intramedullary (conventional) osteosarcoma
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Classic form; spindle cell or pleomorphic tumor producing osteoid. Most common type.
osteosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: TP53, RB1 mutations; Li-Fraumeni, hereditary retinoblastoma [17-22].

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: Low-grade intramedullary osteosarcoma
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Well-differentiated, less aggressive. Rare metastatic potential.
osteosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: Similar sites as conventional but low-grade behavior.

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: Parosteal osteosarcoma
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Low-grade juxtacortical variant. Most common in posterior distal femur. Very low metastatic potential. Dedifferentiation in 24–43% of cases [423,424].
osteosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: Wide excision definitive; chemotherapy not routinely indicated.

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: Periosteal osteosarcoma
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Intermediate-grade juxtacortical lesion with cartilaginous differentiation. Usually femoral or tibial [422].
osteosarcoma_subtypes.key_associations: May consider chemotherapy; no proven survival benefit [472-474].

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: High-grade surface osteosarcoma
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Rare, high-grade juxtacortical variant. Aggressive behavior requiring multimodality treatment.

osteosarcoma_subtypes.name: High-grade undifferentiated pleomorphic sarcoma (UPS) of bone
osteosarcoma_subtypes.description: Previously called malignant fibrous histiocytoma (MFH) of bone. High-grade, arises in appendicular skeleton. High risk of local recurrence and metastasis [518].

tier_2_handbook.osteosarcoma_molecular_pathogenesis: Osteosarcoma is associated with complex genomic alterations. Nearly 70% of patients exhibit mutations in the retinoblastoma gene RB1 [443]. TP53 germline mutations underlie Li-Fraumeni syndrome, which confers a high risk of osteosarcoma [17-19]. Hereditary retinoblastoma (RB1 germline mutation) increases risk 300–600 times [23]. Other genetic predisposition syndromes include Rothmund-Thomson syndrome type 2, Bloom syndrome, Werner syndrome, RAPADILINO syndrome, and Diamond-Blackfan anemia [22]. Radiation exposure is also a risk factor [24,25]. Multiple chromosomal aberrations are common, with no single pathognomonic fusion. Approximately 70% show clonal abnormalities.
tier_2_handbook.osteosarcoma_epidemiology: Osteosarcoma accounts for ~25% of primary bone cancers in the United States [3]. It has a bimodal age distribution: first peak at 10–14 years (during adolescent growth spurt) and second peak after age 65 (often secondary to Paget disease or prior radiation) [420]. In children and adolescents, osteosarcoma is second most common primary bone cancer after Ewing sarcoma [4]. Five-year relative survival for non-metastatic osteosarcoma is approximately 60–70% with modern chemotherapy [28-30]. Survival for metastatic disease at presentation is much lower (approx 20–30%). Incidence is slightly higher in males. Race-specific SEER data not explicitly provided in source.

osteosarcoma_risk_factors.detail: High risk of multiple cancers including osteosarcoma; TP53 mutation [17-19].
Risk of osteosarcoma 300–600 times higher than general population [20-23].
Rothmund-Thomson, Bloom syndrome, Werner syndrome, RAPADILINO, Diamond-Blackfan anemia [22].
Most common radiation-induced bone sarcoma; risk increases with dose [24,25].
Osteosarcoma can arise in pagetic bone in older adults [20].

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Bone cancer presentations vary substantially by histologic subtype. Chondrosarcoma, the most common primary bone cancer in adults (>40% of cases), typically presents with mild, insidious pain and swelling, often in the pelvis or proximal femur, and may be discovered late when tumors become large [MS-6]. Chordoma arises from notochordal remnants in the axial skeleton, most commonly the sacrum (50–60%), skull base (25–35%), and mobile spine; symptoms include deep localized pain, radiculopathy, and for cervical chordomas, airway obstruction or dysphagia [MS-11]. Ewing sarcoma occurs predominantly in adolescents and young adults, presenting with localized pain or swelling, occasional constitutional symptoms such as fever, weight loss, and fatigue, and may show an elevated serum LDH and leukocytosis [MS-16]. Giant cell tumor of bone (GCTB) usually occurs between ages 20 and 40, most often in the meta-epiphyseal region of the distal femur or proximal tibia, with pain and swelling; it is benign but locally aggressive and can metastasize to lungs [MS-23]. Osteosarcoma, the most common primary malignant bone tumor in children and young adults (bimodal age distribution, peaks at 10–14 years and >65 years), presents with pain and swelling, often intermittent initially, and may be confused with growing pains or injury; it spreads hematogenously, most commonly to lung [MS-26–MS-27].

symptoms.name: Pain (all subtypes)
symptoms.detail: Localized pain, often deep and aching, may be intermittent early and become constant. For pelvic/axial chondrosarcoma, pain has an insidious onset [MS-7]. Osteosarcoma pain can be mistaken for growing pains or trauma [MS-27]. Chordoma pain is localized deep pain or radiculopathy; cervical chordoma may cause dysphagia [MS-11].
symptoms.alarm: Nocturnal pain, pain not relieved by rest, progressive worsening.

symptoms.name: Swelling or mass (all subtypes)
symptoms.detail: Palpable mass over affected bone, may be tender. In Ewing sarcoma, periosteal reaction can produce 'onion skin' appearance on imaging [MS-16]. In GCTB, swelling over meta-epiphyseal region.
symptoms.alarm: Rapid growth, erythema, warmth suggesting aggressive behavior.

symptoms.name: Constitutional symptoms (Ewing sarcoma)
symptoms.detail: Fever, weight loss, fatigue, occasionally present at diagnosis [MS-16].
symptoms.alarm: Fever without infection, unexplained weight loss.

symptoms.name: Neurologic symptoms (chordoma, spine tumors)
symptoms.detail: Radiculopathy, paresthesias, motor deficits, cauda equina syndrome in sacral chordoma; cranial nerve deficits in skull base chordoma [MS-11, MS-26].

symptoms.name: Pathologic fracture (osteosarcoma, GCTB)
symptoms.detail: Fracture through weakened bone, may be presenting event.

signs.name: Palpable mass
signs.detail: Firm, fixed, may be tender. In Ewing sarcoma, often warm; in osteosarcoma, may be associated with joint effusion.

signs.name: Limited range of motion
signs.detail: Joint stiffness due to tumor proximity; common in juxta-articular osteosarcoma and GCTB.

signs.name: Neurologic deficit
signs.detail: Motor or sensory loss associated with spinal chordoma or sacral tumors; cranial nerve palsy in skull base chordoma [MS-11].

signs.name: Periosteal reaction (Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma)
signs.detail: 'Onion skin' layering on radiographs in Ewing sarcoma; 'sunburst' appearance in osteosarcoma.

signs.name: Cutaneous changes
signs.detail: Erythema, warmth over mass, occasionally dilated veins (especially in osteosarcoma).

atypical_or_missed: Pain attributed to growing pains in children with osteosarcoma delaying diagnosis [MS-27].
Pelvic/axial chondrosarcoma often remains asymptomatic until large due to insidious pain [MS-7].
GCTB may be misdiagnosed as brown tumor of hyperparathyroidism [GCTB-1, MS-24].
Chordoma symptoms may mimic degenerative spine disease or herniated disc.
Ewing sarcoma constitutional symptoms may be mistaken for infection.

red_flags: Nocturnal pain or pain at rest, especially if progressive.
Neurologic deficits: bowel/bladder dysfunction, extremity weakness, cranial nerve palsy.
Rapidly enlarging mass, especially with erythema or warmth.
Unexplained fever, weight loss, fatigue (Ewing sarcoma).
Bone lesion in a patient with known cancer predisposition syndrome (Li-Fraumeni, hereditary retinoblastoma, Rothmund-Thomson, etc.) [MS-5, MS-28].
Lesion > 2 cm cartilage cap on imaging in a pre-existing osteochondroma suggests malignant transformation [MS-7].
Elevated LDH or alkaline phosphatase in a patient with bone pain.

diagnostic.rationale: Initial imaging for any symptomatic bone lesion; reveals cortical destruction, periosteal reaction, matrix mineralization.
diagnostic.key_findings: Poorly marginated lesion, 'onion skin' (Ewing sarcoma), 'sunburst' (osteosarcoma), 'ring-and-arc' calcification (chondrosarcoma), lytic lesion (GCTB).

diagnostic.test: Cross-sectional imaging – contrast-enhanced MRI of primary site (whole bone)
diagnostic.rationale: Best modality to define intramedullary and soft tissue extent, detect skip metastases, evaluate neurovascular involvement. Preferred for operative planning [BONE-1, BONE-A, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Tumor extent, medullary involvement, extraosseous extension, joint involvement, skip lesions.

diagnostic.test: CT scan of primary site (with contrast if MRI contraindicated)
diagnostic.rationale: Alternative to MRI; delineates cortical destruction and calcifications.
diagnostic.key_findings: Matrix mineralization, cortical breach, pathologic fracture.

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for staging because lung is most common metastatic site for most bone sarcomas (osteosarcoma, Ewing sarcoma, chondrosarcoma) [BONE-1, EW-1, OSTEO-1].
Confirm diagnosis and histologic grade prior to any surgical procedure or fixation. Must be performed at treating institution. Biopsy tract must be placed in future resection bed [BONE-A, MS-5].
diagnostic.key_findings: Specific histologic type, grade, IHC, molecular diagnostics.

diagnostic.test: Laboratory studies: CBC, CMP (including calcium), LDH, alkaline phosphatase
diagnostic.rationale: Baseline labs; LDH and ALP have prognostic significance in osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma; calcium assesses for hypercalcemia [BONE-1, BONE-A, MS-4].
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated LDH (worse prognosis), elevated ALP (osteosarcoma), leukocytosis (Ewing sarcoma).

diagnostic.rationale: Perform when multiple myeloma suspected (especially in patients ≥40 with lytic lesions) [BONE-1].

staging.test: FDG-PET/CT (head-to-toe) (preferred for Ewing sarcoma; category 2B for general workup)
staging.rationale: Detects distant metastases and assesses metabolic activity; preferred for Ewing sarcoma staging and restaging [EW-1, OSTEO-1].
staging.indication: All bone sarcomas, particularly Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma; consider for chordoma [CHOR-1].

staging.rationale: Detects skeletal metastases and skip lesions. May be used if FDG-PET/CT is negative [BONE-1, CHOR-1, OSTEO-1].
staging.indication: Complementary to PET/CT; optional for GCTB [GCTB-1].
When multiple myeloma is in differential.

staging.rationale: Routine staging for metastasis; part of standard workup for chordoma and in patients ≥40 [BONE-1, CHOR-1].
To detect additional chordoma lesions; recommended for chordoma workup [CHOR-1].

staging.test: Bone marrow biopsy and/or screening MRI spine/pelvis
staging.rationale: Consider for Ewing sarcoma to evaluate bone marrow involvement [EW-1].

staging.test: MRI (with and without contrast) or CT of skeletal metastatic sites
staging.rationale: Detailed imaging of suspicious metastatic lesions identified on primary imaging [OSTEO-1].

biomarkers.rationale: Identify t(11;22)(q24;q12) EWSR1-FLI1 translocation (85% of cases) or other fusions; 90% have one of four specific chromosome translocations [EW-1, MS-15].

biomarkers.test: Multigene panel testing (MGPT) with validated/FDA-approved assay
biomarkers.rationale: To identify targeted therapy opportunities (e.g., IDH1 mutations in chondrosarcoma, actionable alterations in chordoma, Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma) [CHON-4, CHOR-3, EW-3, OSTEO-3, MS-34].
biomarkers.when: Consider for metastatic or recurrent disease; for Ewing sarcoma if initial PCR/FISH/cytogenetics negative.

biomarkers.test: Tumor mutational burden (TMB) and mismatch repair/microsatellite instability (MMR/MSI) testing
biomarkers.rationale: To inform immunotherapy options (pembrolizumab for MSI-H/dMMR; pembrolizumab or nivolumab/ipilimumab for TMB-H ≥10 mut/Mb) [CHON-4, CHOR-3, EW-3, OSTEO-3, MS-35].
biomarkers.when: Consider for metastatic chondrosarcoma, recurrent chordoma, metastatic Ewing sarcoma, unresectable/metastatic osteosarcoma.

biomarkers.rationale: Ivosidenib is an option for susceptible IDH1 mutations in conventional chondrosarcoma grades 1–3 [BONE-B 1 of 7, MS-9].
biomarkers.when: For advanced or metastatic conventional chondrosarcoma.

biomarkers.rationale: Select patients with family history may have genetic predisposition (Li-Fraumeni, RB1, Rothmund-Thomson, etc.) [BONE-A, MS-28].

do_not_miss: Core needle or open biopsy prior to any surgical procedure or fixation [BONE-A].
Preoperative biopsy consultation with orthopedic oncologist regarding prebiopsy imaging [BONE-A].
Fresh tissue for biomarker testing and tissue banking if clinically indicated [BONE-A].
Fertility consultation before initiating chemotherapy in patients of child-bearing potential [BONE-A, EW-1, OSTEO-1].
Screening MRI of spinal axis for chordoma [CHOR-1].
Chest CT for staging all bone sarcomas.
avoid: Fine-needle aspiration (FNA) for primary bone tumor diagnosis – insufficient tissue, risk of nondiagnostic result [MS-5].
Biopsy at a non-specialized center; should be performed at the institution that will do definitive management [BONE-A].
Strong acid-based decalcification (other than EDTA or EDTA + formic acid) for specimens requiring molecular testing [BONE-D 1 of 2].
Do not skip FDG-PET/CT for Ewing sarcoma staging; it is preferred [EW-1].
Do not use whole-body MRI as sole staging for bone sarcomas unless part of a clinical trial (noninferiority not established) [MS-16].

staging.system: AJCC 8th Edition (2017) TNM Staging System for Bone (primary malignant lymphoma and multiple myeloma are not included) [ST-1, ST-2].

t_stages.description: Primary tumor cannot be assessed
No evidence of primary tumor

t_stages.description: Tumor ≤ 8 cm in greatest dimension

t_stages.stage: Appendicular Skeleton, Trunk, Skull, and Facial Bones - T2
t_stages.description: Tumor > 8 cm in greatest dimension

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed (may be considered N0 unless clinically evident)

stage_groups.criteria: T1, N0, M0, G1 or GX
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low-grade, small tumor (≤8 cm) without metastases.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgical resection with wide margins, adjuvant therapy as indicated.

stage_groups.criteria: T2, N0, M0, G1 or GX; OR T3, N0, M0, G1 or GX
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Low-grade, large tumor (>8 cm) or discontinuous tumors, no metastases.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – surgery plus possible RT/systemic therapy.

stage_groups.criteria: T1, N0, M0, G2 or G3
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade (moderately or poorly differentiated), small tumor (≤8 cm), no metastases.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative – neoadjuvant chemotherapy, wide excision, adjuvant chemotherapy.

stage_groups.criteria: T2, N0, M0, G2 or G3
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade, large tumor (>8 cm), no metastases.

stage_groups.criteria: T3, N0, M0, G2 or G3
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: High-grade, discontinuous tumors in primary bone site, no distant metastases.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N0, M1a, any G
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Potentially curative if oligometastatic and resectable; systemic therapy + local control to primary and metastases.

stage_groups.criteria: Any T, N1, any M, any G; OR Any T, any N, M1b, any G
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Any grade/size, regional lymph node involvement (N1) and/or bone/other distant metastases (M1b).
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative or potentially curative in selected cases; systemic therapy, local control as feasible.

staging_pearls: Histologic grade (G) is critical: G1 (well differentiated, low grade), G2 (moderately differentiated, high grade), G3 (poorly differentiated, high grade). GX is used if grade cannot be assessed. G1 and GX are considered low grade for staging purposes [ST-2].
There are no AJCC prognostic stage groupings specifically for spine and pelvis; separate T definitions apply [ST-1, ST-2].
Because lymph node involvement is rare in bone sarcomas, NX designation may not be appropriate; cases should be considered N0 unless clinical node involvement is unequivocally evident [ST-1].
M1a specifically denotes lung metastasis; M1b denotes bone or other distant sites (including non-lung visceral metastases) [ST-2].
Staging applies to all primary malignant bone tumors except primary malignant lymphoma and multiple myeloma [ST-1].
The 8th edition continues to use the TNM and histologic grade to define prognostic groups; stage groups for appendicular skeleton, trunk, skull, and facial bones are as defined in Table 2 [ST-2].

management_principles.overview: Primary bone cancers are rare neoplasms (~0.2% of all cancers) with wide clinical heterogeneity. In 2025, an estimated 3770 new cases and 2190 deaths are expected in the United States [MS-2]. Multimodal therapy involving surgery, chemotherapy, and radiation is central to management. For osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma, multiagent chemotherapy (neoadjuvant and adjuvant) has markedly improved prognosis, with 60-70% of non-metastatic osteosarcoma patients cured and approximately 82% 5-year relative survival for localized Ewing sarcoma [MS-2]. The treatment philosophy emphasizes a multidisciplinary team approach, limb-sparing surgery when possible, and long-term survivorship care including surveillance for late effects of treatment. Choice of therapy is driven by histologic subtype, grade, tumor location, resectability, metastatic status, and molecular markers (e.g., IDH1 mutations, MSI/MMR status, TMB). The guidelines stress that all recommendations are category 2A unless otherwise noted [CAT-1].

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with resectable primary tumors without distant metastases
by_intent.approach: Surgery (wide excision or intralesional excision depending on tumor type) ± neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy ± radiation therapy, with goal of negative margins and local control.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with metastatic or unresectable disease
by_intent.approach: Systemic therapy (chemotherapy, targeted therapy, immunotherapy), radiation for symptom control, and/or surgical excision of oligometastases. Clinical trials encouraged.

by_intent.patient_group: Patients with high-grade osteosarcoma or Ewing sarcoma prior to surgery
by_intent.approach: Preoperative chemotherapy (category 1 for both) to downstage tumor and facilitate resection; histologic response provides prognostic information.

management_principles.mdt_requirements: Primary bone tumors should be evaluated and treated by a multidisciplinary team with expertise in these tumors. Core team includes orthopedic oncologist, bone pathologist, medical/pediatric oncologist, radiation oncologist, musculoskeletal radiologist. Additional specialists: thoracic surgeon, plastic surgeon, interventional radiologist, physiatrist, vascular/general surgeon, neurosurgeon/orthopedic spine surgeon, palliative care physician, and other surgical subspecialties as clinically indicated [TEAM-1]. The NCCN panel recommends care delivered directly by the multidisciplinary team (category 1) [BONE-A].
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Not explicitly defined in the guideline. Clinicians should use clinical judgment. The regorafenib trial for metastatic osteosarcoma required ECOG performance status 0–1 [MS-32]. For high-dose methotrexate regimens, MAP is preferred in patients less than 40 years with excellent performance status [BONE-B4]. Performance status and patient fitness are considered in selecting appropriate treatment intensity.

options.label: Intralesional excision ± surgical adjuvant (e.g., cryosurgery)
Observation

management_pathways.title: Chondrosarcoma - Low-Grade Extracompartmental Appendicular, Grade I Axial, High-Grade (II, III), Clear Cell, or Extracompartmental

options.label: Wide excision (limb-sparing or amputation) to achieve negative margins
RT (preoperative or postoperative) for borderline resectable/unresectable

adjuvant_logic.summary: Negative margins: no RT needed. Microscopically positive margins: consider RT (70 Gy). Gross positive margins: RT >70 Gy. For high-grade/dedifferentiated: consider postoperative RT even with negative margins? Not explicitly stated; RT for positive margins [BONE-C1].

options.label: Definitive RT (>70 Gy with specialized techniques)
Consider osteosarcoma regimens (e.g., cisplatin/doxorubicin, MAP) – category 2B preferred
Ivosidenib for susceptible IDH1 mutations
Pazopanib, Sunitinib, Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab, Sunitinib + Nivolumab

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy for metastatic/widespread disease; role in neoadjuvant not established.

options.label: Ewing sarcoma regimens (VDC-IE, VAIA, VIDE) – category 2B
Doxorubicin monotherapy or Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna

adjuvant_logic.summary: Systemic therapy options for primary or metastatic disease.

options.label: Surgical excision of all sites if possible
Radiation for unresectable sites
Consider clinical trial
Systemic therapy according to subtype (see BONE-B1)
RT, surgery, or ablative therapies for symptomatic sites
Consider clinical trial

management_pathways.title: Chordoma - Conventional (including chondroid) – Sacrococcygeal and Mobile Spine

options.label: Wide excision ± adjuvant RT (preoperative, intraoperative, and/or postoperative)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider adjuvant RT for positive surgical margins or large extracompartmental tumors. For microscopically positive margins: 70 Gy; for gross positive margins: 72-78 Gy using specialized techniques [BONE-C2].

options.label: Definitive RT (>70 Gy with specialized techniques – proton, carbon ion, IMRT)

management_pathways.title: Chordoma - Conventional (including chondroid) – Skull Base / Clival

options.label: Intralesional excision (maximal safe excision) ± adjuvant RT

adjuvant_logic.summary: Consider adjuvant RT for positive surgical margins or large extracompartmental tumors. Consider re-excision if necessary. Follow-up contrast-enhanced MRI to assess adequacy of excision [CHOR-2]. Postoperative RT >70 Gy for positive margins using specialized techniques [BONE-C2].

options.label: Definitive RT (>70 Gy with specialized techniques)
Treat per NCCN Guidelines for Soft Tissue Sarcoma
Multiagent chemotherapy for at least 9 weeks prior to local therapy (category 1). Preferred: VDC-IE (vincristine/doxorubicin/cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide/etoposide). Alternatives: VAIA, VIDE.
Wide excision (limb-sparing or amputation), or definitive RT (concurrent with chemo), or amputation in selected cases.

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue chemotherapy post-local control for 28-49 weeks (category 1). For negative margins: chemotherapy alone, consider RT only for pelvic tumors. For positive margins: RT + chemotherapy. For close margins: RT may be considered [EW-2]. Definitive RT followed by chemotherapy (category 1).

options.label: Consider RT and/or surgery to primary site for local control/palliation
Chemotherapy (second-line) or best supportive care

Primary therapy: multiagent chemotherapy (VAIA, VDC, VDC-IE, VIDE) for at least 9 weeks. Longer treatment prior to local control may be considered based on response. Select patients with impending pathologic fracture or fungation may benefit from immediate surgery [EW-1].
Wide excision or definitive RT to primary site; local control to metastases if oligometastatic (excision or RT).

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue chemotherapy (category 1) for 28-49 weeks. For pulmonary metastases: complete response → consider whole lung irradiation (WLI) or excision; partial response → excision ± WLI. For widely metastatic: continuing chemotherapy, palliative RT, or other techniques [EW-3]. Consider MGPT and TMB/MMR testing.

options.label: Second-line chemotherapy, RT, and/or surgery; best supportive care; clinical trial

nodes.criteria: Low-grade osteosarcoma (intramedullary or surface) or periosteal osteosarcoma

options.label: Wide excision (primary treatment)

adjuvant_logic.summary: If final pathology reveals high-grade, then adjuvant chemotherapy (category 1) is indicated. For periosteal, chemotherapy may be considered but no proven survival benefit [OSTEO-1, MS-29].

management_pathways.title: Osteosarcoma – High-Grade (Intramedullary + Surface) and Undifferentiated Pleomorphic Sarcoma (UPS) of Bone

options.label: Perioperative chemotherapy (category 1; preoperative preferred). Preferred regimens: Cisplatin/Doxorubicin (category 1), MAP (high-dose methotrexate/leucovorin/doxorubicin/cisplatin) (category 1, preferred for patients <40 years with excellent PS). Other: Cisplatin/Doxorubicin/Ifosfamide/Mesna/High-Dose Methotrexate/Leucovorin.
Wide excision

adjuvant_logic.summary: Good response (<10% viable tumor): continue same preoperative regimen. Poor response (≥10% viable tumor): continue same regimen or consider changing chemotherapy (category 3). Positive margins: consider additional local therapy (re-excision or RT). Unresectable: RT or chemotherapy alone. UPS of bone: follow osteosarcoma regimens (category 2B) [BONE-B4].

options.label: Chemotherapy (cisplatin/doxorubicin or MAP as first-line), then reassess primary and metastases for resectability.
Metastasectomy (for pulmonary, visceral, skeletal)
Stereotactic RT (if metastasectomy not possible) or ablation

adjuvant_logic.summary: Continue chemotherapy. If unresectable: reassess primary for local control; consider RT or chemotherapy alone [OSTEO-3].

options.label: Chemotherapy
RT (including radiopharmaceuticals like radium-223)

management_pathways.title: Giant Cell Tumor of Bone – Localized Disease

options.label: Excision (intralesional with effective adjuvant may be adequate)

nodes.criteria: Resectable with unacceptable morbidity and/or unresectable axial lesions

options.label: Denosumab (preferred)
Serial embolization (preferred)
RT (50-60 Gy; may be associated with increased risk of malignant transformation)

adjuvant_logic.summary: Imaging to assess response. If stable/improved with incomplete healing: if becomes resectable, consider excision; if remains unresectable, continue denosumab, embolization, or RT. Denosumab may be continued until progression in responding disease. Long-term denosumab may increase local recurrence risk [GCTB-2].

management_pathways.title: Giant Cell Tumor of Bone – Metastatic at Presentation

nodes.criteria: Metastatic disease at presentation, primary tumor treatable per localized algorithm

options.label: Consider excision of metastases
Denosumab (preferred)
RT
Observation

Second-line chemotherapy: Cyclophosphamide/Topotecan (preferred), Irinotecan/Temozolomide (preferred), Irinotecan/Temozolomide/Vincristine (preferred). Other: Cabozantinib, Docetaxel/Gemcitabine, High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna, Regorafenib. Useful in certain circumstances: ICE, Lurbinectedin (category 2B).
RT ± surgery for local control/palliation
Clinical trial

Second-line therapy: Etoposide/High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna (preferred), High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna (preferred), Regorafenib (category 1 preferred), Sorafenib (preferred). Other: Cabozantinib, Cyclophosphamide/Topotecan, Docetaxel/Gemcitabine, Gemcitabine, Everolimus/Sorafenib (category 2B). Useful in certain circumstances: Cyclophosphamide/Mesna/Etoposide, ICE, High-Dose Methotrexate/Leucovorin-based regimens, Radiopharmaceuticals including radium-223 beyond second-line.
Excision if possible
RT (including radiopharmaceuticals like radium-223) or Embolization/Ablation
Best supportive care
Clinical trial

management_pathways.title: Biomarker-Guided Treatment Options for Unresectable/Metastatic Disease

nodes.criteria: MSI-H/dMMR (any histology except GCTB), progressed after prior treatment, no satisfactory alternative

options.label: Pembrolizumab (preferred) [BONE-B1]

nodes.criteria: TMB-H (≥10 mut/Mb), unresectable/metastatic, progressed after prior treatment, no satisfactory alternative (not for GCTB)

options.label: Pembrolizumab (useful in certain circumstances) or Ipilimumab + Nivolumab (useful in certain circumstances) [BONE-B1]

nodes.criteria: Chondrosarcoma with susceptible IDH1 mutation (conventional grades 1–3 or dedifferentiated)

options.label: Ivosidenib
Lapatinib [BONE-B2]

nodes.criteria: All metastatic/unresectable bone sarcomas (except GCTB) – consider MGPT, TMB, and MMR/MSI testing

options.label: Multigene panel testing (MGPT) with validated/FDA-approved assay to determine targeted therapy opportunities. TMB and MMR/MSI testing for immunotherapy eligibility.

items.text: Initial plain radiograph of symptomatic bone lesion to identify aggressive features.
Contrast-enhanced MRI (or CT if MRI contraindicated) of primary site to assess intramedullary extent, soft tissue involvement, and neurovascular relationships.
Chest imaging: chest CT with or without contrast (non-contrast low-dose CT recommended for restaging). For some tumors, chest x-ray may be used.
Whole-body staging: FDG-PET/CT (head-to-toe, preferred for Ewing sarcoma) and/or bone scan. For chordoma: skull base to midthigh FDG-PET/CT or bone scan if negative.
items.recommendation: Category 2A (FDG-PET/CT for bone scan: category 2B in initial bone lesion workup [BONE-1])

items.text: Screening MRI of spinal axis for chordoma.
For osteosarcoma: MRI (with and without contrast) or CT of skeletal metastatic sites if abnormalities on primary imaging.
Complete blood count (CBC) and comprehensive metabolic panel (CMP) with calcium (to assess hypercalcemia).
Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) – prognostic value for osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma.
Serum protein electrophoresis (SPEP) if multiple myeloma suspected (age ≥40).
Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) and mammogram as indicated for workup of bone metastasis in patients ≥40.
Biopsy (core needle or open) should be performed at the treating institution, ideally by the surgeon who will perform definitive resection, to ensure biopsy tract lies within planned resection bed.
Pre-biopsy consultation with orthopedic oncologist; with pediatric oncologist for children.

Pathology evaluation by experienced sarcoma pathologist. Ancillary studies: immunohistochemistry, FISH, cytogenetics, and/or molecular testing as needed for Ewing sarcoma (CD99, translocation testing).
Fresh tissue may be needed for biomarker testing and tissue banking.
Decalcification should use solutions preserving RNA and DNA (EDTA or EDTA+formic acid; avoid strong acid).
Fertility consultation should be offered to individuals of childbearing potential before cytotoxic chemotherapy.
Consider genetic consultation for patients with personal/family history suggestive of genetic predisposition for bone sarcomas (e.g., Li-Fraumeni, hereditary retinoblastoma).
All patients should be evaluated by a multidisciplinary team before initiation of therapy. Care delivered directly by team (category 1).

surgery.role: Surgery is the primary local control modality for most bone sarcomas. Wide excision with histologically negative margins optimizes local control. Limb-sparing surgery is preferred when reasonable functional outcomes can be achieved; amputation is reserved for tumors not amenable to limb-sparing or salvage after recurrence. Intralesional excision is acceptable for low-grade lesions (atypical cartilaginous tumors) and giant cell tumor of bone.
principles: Biopsy must precede any surgical procedure or fixation; biopsy tract should be placed within planned resection bed.
Wide excision implies histologically negative margins. Negative margins optimize local control.
Limb-sparing excision preferred over amputation if reasonable function expected.
Final pathologic evaluation should include assessment of surgical margins, tumor size/dimensions, and response to preoperative therapy (e.g., percent necrosis).
Fresh tissue may be needed for biomarker testing and tissue banking.
Consultation with physiatrist for rehabilitation planning.

procedures.name: Wide excision
procedures.approach: En bloc resection with a cuff of normal tissue; margin can be achieved by limb-sparing or amputation.
procedures.indications: All resectable high-grade bone sarcomas (osteosarcoma, chondrosarcoma grade II/III, Ewing sarcoma, chordoma sacrum/spine), low-grade extracompartmental appendicular, axial grade I chondrosarcoma, dedifferentiated and mesenchymal subtypes (after systemic therapy), and recurrent disease when feasible.
procedures.key_complications: Positive margins leading to local recurrence, functional loss, need for amputation, neurovascular injury, infection, nonunion or failure of reconstruction.

procedures.name: Intralesional excision (curettage)
procedures.approach: Piecemeal removal of tumor; often supplemented with surgical adjuvants (e.g., cryosurgery, phenol, cement).
procedures.indications: Atypical cartilaginous tumors (low-grade intracompartmental appendicular), giant cell tumor of bone (intralesional curettage with adjuvant), skull base chordoma (maximal safe intralesional excision).
procedures.key_complications: Local recurrence (higher than with wide excision), tumor spillage, joint destruction if subchondral bone violated.

procedures.name: Amputation
procedures.approach: Removal of limb at a level proximal to the tumor with negative margins.
procedures.indications: Selected cases where limb-sparing surgery not feasible (e.g., extensive tumor involvement of neurovascular bundle, locally recurrent disease after limb-sparing, patient preference).
procedures.key_complications: Phantom limb pain, stump complications, functional limitation, psychological impact.

surgery.resectability_criteria: Resectability is determined by ability to achieve negative margins while preserving critical structures. For Ewing sarcoma, wide excision with negative margins is standard; preoperative RT for marginally resectable tumors. For osteosarcoma, resectable if wide excision possible with acceptable functional outcome. For chordoma, resectable if en bloc excision with negative margins feasible. For giant cell tumor, resectable if complete intralesional excision with acceptable morbidity; unresectable if lesion causes unacceptable morbidity or is axial. The guidelines emphasize multidisciplinary assessment.

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy is used as definitive treatment for unresectable tumors (e.g., chordoma, chondrosarcoma of axial skeleton), as adjuvant therapy after surgery for positive margins or large extracompartmental tumors, and as palliative therapy for metastatic sites. Specialized techniques (IMRT, proton, carbon ion, SRS/SBRT) allow high-dose therapy with normal tissue sparing. Concurrent chemotherapy with RT is used in Ewing sarcoma.
principles: RT should be delivered at the same specialized center providing surgical and systemic interventions.
Specialized techniques (IMRT, particle beam, SRS/SBRT) considered as indicated to maximize normal tissue sparing.
RT doses listed are for conventional fractionation (1.8–2.0 Gy/fraction). Alternative fractionation used for SRS/SBRT.
Preoperative RT (19.8–50.4 Gy) may be considered for marginally resectable tumors, especially in Ewing sarcoma and chordoma.
Postoperative RT should begin within 60 days and is given concurrently with chemotherapy in Ewing sarcoma (withholding anthracyclines during RT per Womer protocol).
For Ewing sarcoma, definitive RT should start by week 12 of VDC/IE or week 18 of VIDE.

dose_frameworks.name: Chondrosarcoma – unresectable low-grade/grade I axial
dose_frameworks.indication: Unresectable low-grade extracompartmental appendicular or grade I axial tumors [BONE-C1]

dose_frameworks.name: Chondrosarcoma – high-grade/clear cell/extracompartmental, postoperative
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 70 Gy (microscopically positive margins); 72–78 Gy (gross positive margins)

dose_frameworks.name: Ewing sarcoma – definitive RT to primary
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 55.8 Gy (cone-down to post-chemotherapy volume; initial 45 Gy to GTV1+margin)
dose_frameworks.fractions: 45 Gy in 25 fractions; total 55.8 Gy in ~31 fractions
dose_frameworks.indication: Definitive treatment for primary tumor, localized Ewing sarcoma [BONE-C3]

dose_frameworks.name: Ewing sarcoma – postoperative RT
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 45 Gy (negative margins, poor response); 45 Gy + cone-down to 55.8 Gy for gross positive margins
dose_frameworks.schedule: Concurrent with chemotherapy; start within 60 days of surgery
dose_frameworks.indication: Positive margins, close margins, or poor histologic response [BONE-C3]

dose_frameworks.name: Ewing sarcoma – whole lung irradiation (WLI)
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 15 Gy (age <14 yr) or 18 Gy (age ≥14 yr) at 1.5 Gy/fraction

dose_frameworks.name: Giant cell tumor of bone – RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Unresectable/progressive/recurrent disease not responding to denosumab or embolization [BONE-C4]

dose_frameworks.name: Osteosarcoma – postoperative RT
dose_frameworks.total_dose: 55 Gy + 9–13 Gy boost (total 64–68 Gy to high-risk sites)

dose_frameworks.name: Osteosarcoma – unresectable disease
dose_frameworks.schedule: Conventional, depending on normal tissue tolerance

approaches.name: Definitive RT (Ewing sarcoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 45 Gy initial + 10.8 Gy cone-down to 55.8 Gy total; concurrent with chemotherapy (withhold anthracyclines during RT)
approaches.indication: Localized Ewing sarcoma, especially unresectable or when surgery would cause significant morbidity
approaches.key_trial: Womer RB, et al. J Clin Oncol 2012;30:4148-4154 [BONE-C5]
approaches.toxicities: Acute skin reactions, mucositis, bone marrow suppression (when combined with chemotherapy), late effects including secondary malignancy, growth disturbance, fibrosis.

approaches.name: Preoperative RT (Ewing sarcoma and selected chordoma/chondrosarcoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 36–45 Gy (Ewing); 19.8–50.4 Gy (chordoma/chondrosarcoma) followed by individualized postoperative dose
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes, for Ewing sarcoma; not typically for chordoma/chondrosarcoma
approaches.indication: Marginally resectable tumors to shrink tumor and improve surgical margin rates
approaches.key_trial: Schuck A, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2005;63:1562-1567 [BONE-C5]

approaches.name: Postoperative RT (all histologies with positive margins)
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes, for Ewing sarcoma; no for other histologies
approaches.indication: Positive or close margins after surgery, large extracompartmental tumors, high-grade/dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
approaches.key_trial: DeLaney TF, et al. J Surg Oncol 2014;110:115-122 [BONE-C5]
approaches.toxicities: Late effects: fibrosis, secondary malignancy, bone necrosis, joint stiffness.

approaches.name: Stereotactic body RT (SBRT) for oligometastases
approaches.dose_fractionation: Variable (e.g., 50 Gy in 4–5 fractions, 54 Gy in 3 fractions)
approaches.indication: Oligometastatic disease from Ewing sarcoma, osteosarcoma, or other bone sarcomas when surgery not feasible
approaches.key_trial: Baumann BC, et al. J Surg Oncol 2016;114:65-69; Mehta N, et al. Sarcoma 2013;360214 [BONE-C5]
approaches.toxicities: Pneumonitis, chest wall pain, rib fracture, radiation fibrosis.

approaches.name: Hemithorax irradiation (Ewing sarcoma)
approaches.dose_fractionation: 15–20 Gy (1.5 Gy/fraction) followed by cone-down to primary site
approaches.concurrent_chemotherapy: Yes, after completion of induction chemotherapy
approaches.indication: Chest wall primaries with extensive ipsilateral pleural involvement [BONE-C3]
approaches.key_trial: Indelicato DJ, et al. Int J Radiat Oncol Biol Phys 2011;81:158-166 [BONE-C5]

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy plays a crucial role in the management of Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma (neoadjuvant/adjuvant and metastatic), and in selected cases of chordoma, chondrosarcoma (especially dedifferentiated and mesenchymal subtypes), and giant cell tumor. The guidelines categorize regimens by histology, line of therapy, and preference (preferred, other recommended, useful in certain circumstances). For Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma, first-line multiagent chemotherapy regimens are category 1. For metastatic/relapsed disease, several targeted and immunotherapeutic options are available based on biomarker testing. An FDA-approved biosimilar is an appropriate substitute for any recommended systemic biologic therapy [BONE-B1].

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Superior event-free survival demonstrated in INT-0091 and confirmed in EE2012 trial. Interval-compressed dosing improves outcomes.

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Ewing sarcoma – first-line metastatic at diagnosis
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Multiagent chemotherapy as primary therapy; no consensus on superiority; choice based on institutional preference and patient factors.
Demonstrated response rates in retrospective and phase II studies.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib, Docetaxel/Gemcitabine, High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna, Regorafenib, ICE (ifosfamide/carboplatin/etoposide), Lurbinectedin (category 2B) [BONE-B3]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cisplatin/Doxorubicin (category 1); MAP (high-dose methotrexate/leucovorin/doxorubicin/cisplatin) (category 1, preferred for <40 years excellent PS) [BONE-B4]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: MAP is standard based on European and American Osteosarcoma Study Group trials; survival benefit established.

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Etoposide/High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna (preferred); High-Dose Ifosfamide/Mesna (preferred); Regorafenib (category 1); Sorafenib (preferred) [BONE-B4]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Regorafenib showed PFS benefit in phase II SARC024 and REGOBONE trials.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cabozantinib, Cyclophosphamide/Topotecan, Docetaxel/Gemcitabine, Gemcitabine, Everolimus/Sorafenib (category 2B), and other less preferred options [BONE-B4]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: No known standard chemotherapy. Useful in certain circumstances: Ivosidenib for IDH1 mutations [BONE-B1]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Conventional chondrosarcoma is generally chemo-resistant; IDH1 inhibition shows disease stabilization.
Dedifferentiated subtype may respond to osteosarcoma-like chemotherapy based on histologic similarity.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Ivosidenib (if IDH1 mutated), Pazopanib, Sunitinib, Pembrolizumab, Nivolumab, Sunitinib+Nivolumab [BONE-B1]

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma shares chemosensitivity with Ewing sarcoma.
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Dactinomycin can substitute for doxorubicin if cardiotoxicity concern [BONE-B1]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: No single preferred; options include Imatinib, Dasatinib, Pembrolizumab, Sunitinib (other recommended) [BONE-B2]
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Targeted therapies (imatinib, dasatinib) show disease control; pembrolizumab active in some chordoma patients (AcSé basket trial).
by_stage_and_setting.alternatives: Cisplatin/Imatinib, Imatinib/Sirolimus, Erlotinib, Lapatinib (EGFR-positive), Sorafenib (useful in certain circumstances) [BONE-B2]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Giant cell tumor of bone – unresectable or resectable with morbidity
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: FDA-approved; phase II trials show tumor response, pain reduction, and surgical downstaging.

key_regimens.name: VDC-IE (Ewing sarcoma first-line)

drugs.name: Vincristine
drugs.day: Day 1 of each cycle (VDC and IE)

key_regimens.cycle_days: VDC and IE alternate every 2 weeks (interval compressed). Total therapy: 28-49 weeks.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, febrile neutropenia, cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide), neurotoxicity (vincristine), secondary malignancies.

key_regimens.name: MAP (Osteosarcoma first-line)
key_regimens.setting: Neoadjuvant/adjuvant for high-grade osteosarcoma (category 1), preferred in patients <40 years with excellent PS
key_regimens.cycle_days: Cycles approximately every 3 weeks. Neoadjuvant: 2-3 cycles; adjuvant: 3-4 cycles.
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Nephrotoxicity (methotrexate, cisplatin), mucositis, myelosuppression, cardiotoxicity (doxorubicin), ototoxicity (cisplatin). Glucarpidase strongly recommended for delayed methotrexate elimination.

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Doxorubicin (Osteosarcoma first-line)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 weeks for up to 6 cycles.

key_regimens.name: Regorafenib (Osteosarcoma second-line)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypertension, hand-foot skin reaction, diarrhea, fatigue, liver function abnormalities.

key_regimens.name: Denosumab (Giant cell tumor of bone)
key_regimens.setting: Unresectable GCTB or resectable with morbidity (preferred)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Hypocalcemia, osteonecrosis of the jaw, atypical femoral fractures.

treatment_response_assessment.title: Assessment of Treatment Response
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response is assessed after neoadjuvant/induction therapy (typically after 9-12 weeks for Ewing sarcoma, after 2-3 cycles for osteosarcoma) and at restaging intervals during surveillance. For locally advanced disease, imaging (MRI, CT, FDG-PET/CT) is used to evaluate tumor size, necrosis, and metabolic activity.
response_logic: For osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma, histologic response is defined as good (<10% viable tumor) or poor (≥10% viable tumor) based on pathologic mapping of the resected specimen [OSTEO-2]. This classification guides adjuvant chemotherapy decisions.
For Ewing sarcoma, complete pathologic necrosis at the margin is considered a negative margin [BONE-D1].
For chordoma and chondrosarcoma, response is assessed by imaging (MRI/CT) and clinical status; there is no established viability threshold for adjuvant therapy decisions.
For giant cell tumor, imaging (plain x-rays, contrast-enhanced CT ± MRI) is used to assess response to denosumab or embolization; progressive vs stable vs improved disease guides further therapy.
For metastatic disease, RECIST criteria are commonly used in clinical trials and practice, but not explicitly mandated by NCCN.
imaging_recommendations: Contrast-enhanced MRI ± CT of primary site for anatomic assessment. For Ewing sarcoma, also include x-rays of primary site.
Chest CT (with or without contrast; low-dose non-contrast preferred for restaging) to evaluate pulmonary metastases.

FDG-PET/CT (head-to-toe) or bone scan may be used for restaging, especially in Ewing sarcoma and osteosarcoma, to detect both local and distant recurrence (category 2B for osteosarcoma surveillance) [OSTEO-4].
Use same imaging technique as in initial workup for consistency.
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: Biopsy is recommended for local recurrence to confirm diagnosis, especially distinguishing malignant transformation (e.g., from atypical cartilaginous tumor to higher grade).

In giant cell tumor, local recurrence should be confirmed with biopsy; if resectable, consider denosumab prior to surgery (but risk of increased local recurrence when used before curettage) [BONE-C3].
For suspected progression or relapse, biopsy may be required if imaging is equivocal; for Ewing sarcoma, re-biopsy may be needed for biomarker testing if initial sample insufficient.

Salvage surgery (wide excision if possible) or RT (including re-irradiation with specialized techniques) are options for local recurrence. Systemic therapy (second-line) is indicated for unresectable or metastatic progression.
For osteosarcoma, radiopharmaceuticals including radium-223 are options for relapsed/refractory disease beyond second-line therapy [OSTEO-4].
